[03:14:08] Whether you're a longtime fan or just
[03:14:10] getting started, there's never been a
[03:14:12] better time to jump into the Pokémon
[03:14:14] trading card game. With thousands of
[03:14:16] cards, endless deck possibilities, and
[03:14:18] an evolving competitive meta, there's
[03:14:21] always something new to experience. If
[03:14:23] you're new, following the action is
[03:14:25] easier than ever. Here's what you need
[03:14:27] to know. Each game starts with a coin
[03:14:30] flip to decide who plays first. Both
[03:14:32] players shuffle their decks, draw seven
[03:14:34] cards, and must begin with at least one
[03:14:37] basic Pokémon. If not, they reshuffle
[03:14:39] and draw again until they find one. Each
[03:14:42] player also sets aside six prize cards,
[03:14:45] which are drawn when knocking out an
[03:14:47] opponent's Pokémon.
[03:14:55] The active spot is where battles happen.
[03:14:57] A Pokémon stays there until it retreats,
[03:14:59] gets switched out, or is knocked out.
[03:15:02] Damage is tracked using dice, usually in
[03:15:05] multiples of 10.
[03:15:08] Special conditions such as poisoned,
[03:15:10] burned, or asleep can be lingering
[03:15:13] effects that last longer than a single
[03:15:15] turn. On the bench, Pokémon can't
[03:15:18] attack, but they're still in play. There
[03:15:21] are four kinds of trainer cards: item,
[03:15:23] supporter, stadium, and Pokémon tool.
[03:15:26] Each one brings unique effects to the
[03:15:28] game, adding new twists and strategies
[03:15:30] to every match.
[03:15:34] To win, a player must draw all six prize
[03:15:36] cards by knocking out opposing Pokémon.
[03:15:39] But that's not the only way to win. With
[03:15:41] so many strategies and surprises,
[03:15:44] anything can happen in the Pokémon TCG.
[03:15:46] If you're ever unsure, just listen to
[03:15:48] our expert casters. They'll break it all
[03:15:50] down.
[03:15:51] &gt;&gt; No ancient card yet. There's two. The
[03:15:53] pair will be discarded. Now,
[03:15:56] &gt;&gt; here we go. This is the comeback. Taking
[03:15:58] four prize cards in one turn. Uh, just

[06:59:06] In the Pokemon TCG, there are some terms
[06:59:08] that you might hear that might not be so
[06:59:11] obvious what they mean at face value.
[06:59:13] So, today, let's take a quick crash
[06:59:15] course on some of them. Toolbox. What is
[06:59:19] a toolbox deck? A toolbox deck is where
[06:59:21] you aim to build a deck that has a wide
[06:59:24] range of attackers, so you always have
[06:59:27] one attacker to use against every other
[06:59:29] deck in format. This in theory should
[06:59:31] help make your matchups more positive in
[06:59:33] your favor. Terrestrial Box is a very
[06:59:36] good example of this right now since you
[06:59:38] play Lily's Cafairy EX for dragon types,
[06:59:41] Till Mask Ogreapon EX for dark types,
[06:59:43] and Wellspring Ogreapon for fire types.
[06:59:46] And for basic attacking decks, you can
[06:59:48] sometimes use Terrapagus EX's Crown Opal
[06:59:50] attack to completely become immune
[06:59:52] against them. And for everything else,
[06:59:54] Topaz Bolt from Pikachu EX normally
[06:59:57] cleans them up. Now, while these kind of
[06:59:59] decks sound unbeatable on paper, they
[07:00:02] often lack consistency since they have
[07:00:04] to play a lot of different energy cards
[07:00:06] and a lot of different Pokemon as well.
[07:00:08] But they normally are very fun to watch.
[07:00:11] Gust. The term Gust goes all the way
[07:00:14] back to base set and has been very
[07:00:16] important to the game ever since. Gust
[07:00:19] relates to cards that swap your
[07:00:21] opponent's active Pokémon. Ghost of Wind
[07:00:23] was the first one of these all the way
[07:00:25] back in base set. But since we have had
[07:00:28] cards like Boss's Orders, Landre, Prime
[07:00:31] Catcher, Guzma, and Lyro GX. All of
[07:00:34] these are known as Gusting cards. As you
[07:00:37] can imagine, these cards are normally
[07:00:38] very powerful and are seen in almost
[07:00:41] every deck in format right now, making
[07:00:44] them a true cornerstone of every format
[07:00:46] they are available in. Safeguard. The
[07:00:50] term safeguard refers to Pokémon that
[07:00:52] have abilities or attacks that make them
[07:00:54] immune to other Pokémon. For example,
[07:00:56] Crystal's ability makes it immune to
[07:00:59] damage from EX Pokémon. And Cornerstone
[07:01:02] Ogreapon's ability makes it immune to
[07:01:04] damage from Pokémon that has an ability.
[07:01:06] So, these could be referred to as
[07:01:08] safeguard Pokémon, even though their
[07:01:10] abilities aren't specifically called
[07:01:12] safeguard, like Mimikyu, for example.
[07:01:15] So, now that we're all clued up to some
[07:01:17] Pokémon jargon, let's see if any of
[07:01:19] these terms will make it onto the show
[07:01:20] this weekend. So, let's get back to the
